text:
Felt very anxious for a while. 

Realised it was a combination of doom-scrolling and caffeine.

So, 10 days ago I deleted Twitter and haven't touched it since (plus reduced coffee to ~60mg caffeine per day)

Huge difference in mental mood.

Only downside. I do like to share my work and life.

So, I added a mini Twitter tool inside my personal OS.

It let's me do the three most important things.

1) Post tweets
2) See tweet stats (nice to have)
3) See replies and interact with them.

That way, I can use Twitter as I want, without seeing all the doom the world has right now.

Took about 2 hours with Ralph.

(This runs on my old MacBook)

text: User feedback is right there. You just need to ask 👏
fullText: User feedback is right there. You just need to ask 👏

quote.text:
How in hell do I talk about my product without sounding salesy?

I think the secret is telling interesting stories about how your thing helps people

And how do you get these stories? Ask your users!

Some time ago, I read @dannypostma's tweet about how he collects feedback from users and thought it was genius – so I copied it.

I built a simple system on my website:

After the second payment, @yadaphone prompts the user to answer a feedback form.

It has only two questions:
1) Give me 2-3 examples of how Yadaphone helped you
2) Would you recommend Yadaphone to a friend, and why

In 2 months, it has given me a database of more than 150 user stories – people constantly telling me how Yadaphone helped them and why they love it

Every day I go through it and see what people are writing

Yesterday, this guy dropped this golden piece of feedback

He gave me 2 super valuable data points:

– he said why my product better is than the competitor: Yadaphone works outside of the US, Google Voice doesn't

– he told me how it helped him while traveling – he could make calls abroad without buying an eSIM

So talk to your users – nobody knows your product better than them (including yourself)

quote.text:
You daily dose of good news: Chemists have finally developed a type of plastic whose lifespan can be programmed - it can be made to break down after days, months or years, depending on the design.

The innovation uses chemical building blocks inspired by natural polymers (like DNA) so once the plastic has served its purpose (for instance packaging), it degrades instead of lingering as waste.

This could make single-use plastics far less harmful - if adopted widely, it flips the script on plastic waste and gives real hope for cleaner material cycles.

text:
Progress in running is so satisfying because what would kill me after 5 minutes and put me straight into zone 5 a year ago now barely gets me into zone 3 

Used to never understand why people enjoy it until I took it serious

Still far to go but coming from someone pretty bad in shape it’s getting there. 

I think one significant addition is not sweating at all anymore in tropical climates where a year ago just walking the dog would make me hot af.

Another would be big reduction in eating unhealthy. Why waste time running to ruin it afterwards with snacks.

text:
LLMs are more like humans. 

Therefore, to rank high in them you need to influence them using psychology (reviews, neuromarketing, copywriting) instead of algorithmic (keyword stuffing, link boosting)

Your job is to basically gaslight them.
fullText:
LLMs are more like humans. 

Therefore, to rank high in them you need to influence them using psychology (reviews, neuromarketing, copywriting) instead of algorithmic (keyword stuffing, link boosting)

Your job is to basically gaslight them.

quote.text:
For the past few days, I've been trying to figure out ways to optimize LLMs to shoot traffic at your website. 

AI is looking for "trust" and organic signals that make recommending your brand worthwhile.

In practicality, this looks like places where:

1. ai is allowed to scrape (amazon just banned scraping so thats a no go now)
2. people are having real reviews and discussion

There are 3 places where this fits the bill in a big way:

1. Reddit
2. YouTube
3. Reviews and UGC

I think a lot of the early winners will actually be marketplaces like Target(dot)com, etc.

These are high trust places with a lot of reviews.

shoutout @ttagrin - i asked him all of this and he answered. their team has been spending this year gathering data on this

quote.text:
I noticed slight burn-out patterns in myself again this week. 

Cranky, unable to do non-work relative things without feeling guilty. 

Forced myself to read a book during day-time outside and every 5 minutes my mind would come up with a reason to grab my laptop and work. 

Funny how the mind works. 

I messed up my mind very badly last year so these come back pretty fast, even without crazy work hours.

I do know the sythomes though, so time to slow down a little.

text:
Did you know travel and booking websites like SkyScanner sometimes make their searches a bit slower on purpose?

This way, people feel the site is really “working hard” to find the best deals. If results show up too quickly, users might think the search wasn’t done properly. 

A short wait makes it feel more trustworthy.
fullText:

This way, people feel the site is really “working hard” to find the best deals. If results show up too quickly, users might think the search wasn’t done properly. 

A

text:
The first job I ever got was in a supermarket.

My mom was so done with hearing me scream while playing Counter-Strike: Source all summer that she applied for a job for me when I was 14 years old. 

Guess the owner really saw she was done with geeky me. I got a call the next day to come in for an interview.

I went from "spiegelen" which means pulling all the products to the front to make the shelves look nice at 14, all the way to leading the evening shift with 10 teenagers under me at 19.

Still wild I worked all Saturday for $2.50 at the start.

Great character development.

text:
Been reading Red Rising for the last few days and every time something feels off. 

It feels slightly childish? Simple?

Been trying to figure out what it is, until I realised the major difference. 

It’s written in 1st person, while all others books I read before were in 3rd, which makes a huge difference in story telling.

Great book btw, but I need to get used to the different style.

text: Idk if AI can do large features, I care that I don't understand what it produces when it gets too large and you start to have a code base you don't have a clue how it works.
fullText: Idk if AI can do large features, I care that I don't understand what it produces when it gets too large and you start to have a code base you don't have a clue how it works.

quote.text:
> Large feature

Dump all the context into a smart model (o3, opus), make sure there's examples of structure, key architectural code/organization, then ask the AI to suggest 3 different approaches into a XX_PLAN.md, review, iterate, until happy then create a task list.

AI can do a lot more than yall give it credit for, this approach is still way faster than writing the code manually, you'll end up with the same result eitherway

text:
This 20-day trip taught me to be less scared of the world. 

I went alone. No friends. Just to feel being utterly helpless. 

My bike broke every day, landslides blocked roads and had to drive at night with rain and without headlights. 

No matter the challenge, there would always be a friendly local to help me out. 

After that, you realise being worried about the little things doesn’t matter.

text: Supersonic flying would make my life so much easier. Living a day flying away from your parents sucks. Cutting that in half would be amazing.
fullText: Supersonic flying would make my life so much easier. Living a day flying away from your parents sucks. Cutting that in half would be amazing.

quote.text:
Supersonic is back, baby!

On January 28th, Geppetto broke the sound barrier aboard the first privately-developed supersonic jet.
And today…@realDonaldTrump broke the sound barrier…permanently! 

In 1973, a ban was placed on supersonic flight over the US. It was supposed to protect the public from sonic booms. But instead it banned innovation. The result crippled progress for half a century. The trend of ever-increasing flight speeds ground to a halt.

However, today we can celebrate–quiet supersonic flight is allowed!

The supersonic race is on and a new era of commercial flight can begin. Unleashed, my bet is very much on 🇺🇸(and @boomsupersonic).

Please join me in thanking @realDonaldTrump and @USDOT @SecDuffy for their leadership—as well as @SenTedBuddNC and @RepTroyNehls for introducing the bipartisan SAM act. And to the team @FAANews for leading us in the next steps for boomless flight!
Make America Supersonic!!!
